content.text: *"As for me and my household, we will serve the Lord."*{Joshua 24:15}{The sequence is the foundation upon which all of analysis is built.}{Augustin-Louis Cauchy}

Sequences are ordered lists of objects, fundamental to discrete 
mathematics and computer science. Summations provide compact notation 
for adding many terms.

content.title: Sequence
content.content:
A **sequence** is a function from a subset of the 
integers (usually $\N$ or $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$) to a set $S$. We write 
$\{a_n\}$ or $(a_n)$ for a sequence, where $a_n$ denotes the $n$th term.

content.title: Sequences
content.problem:
1. The sequence $\{a_n\} = \{2, 4, 6, 8, \ldots\}$ with $a_n = 2n$.

2. The Fibonacci sequence: $1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, \ldots$ where each 
 term is the sum of the two preceding terms.

3. The sequence $\{(-1)^n\} = \{-1, 1, -1, 1, \ldots\}$.

items: **Recurrence relation**: $a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2}$ with initial conditions

content.title: Arithmetic Sequence
content.content:
An **arithmetic sequence** has a constant 
difference $d$ between consecutive terms:
\[ a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d \]

content.title: Arithmetic sequence
content.problem:
The sequence $3, 7, 11, 15, 19, \ldots$ is arithmetic with $a_1 = 3$ and 
$d = 4$. The general term is $a_n = 3 + 4(n-1) = 4n - 1$.

content.title: Geometric Sequence
content.content:
A **geometric sequence** has a constant 
ratio $r$ between consecutive terms:
\[ a_n = a_1 \cdot r^{n-1} \]

content.title: Geometric sequence
content.problem:
The sequence $2, 6, 18, 54, \ldots$ is geometric with $a_1 = 2$ and $r = 3$. 
The general term is $a_n = 2 \cdot 3^{n-1}$.

content.title: Summation
content.content:
The **summation** (or sigma notation) represents 
the sum of terms:
\[ \sum_{i=m}^{n} a_i = a_m + a_{m+1} + a_{m+2} + \cdots + a_n \]
The variable $i$ is the **index**, $m$ is the **lower limit**, 
and $n$ is the **upper limit**.

content.title: Carl Friedrich Gauss (1777--1855)
content.content:
As a schoolboy, Gauss allegedly discovered formula eq:sum-integers 
when his teacher asked the class to sum the integers from 1 to 100. While 
others labored, Gauss recognized that pairing $1 + 100$, $2 + 99$, etc., 
gives 50 pairs of 101, yielding $50 \times 101 = 5050$. Gauss became one 
of the greatest mathematicians in history, making profound contributions 
to number theory, geometry, and physics.

content.title: Product Notation
content.content:
The **product notation** represents the 
product of terms:
\[ \prod_{i=m}^{n} a_i = a_m \cdot a_{m+1} \cdot a_{m+2} \cdots a_n \]

content.title: Factorial
content.problem:
The **factorial** $n!$ is defined as:
\[ n! = \prod_{i=1}^{n} i = 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdots n \]
By convention, $0! = 1$.

Examples: $4! = 24$, $5! = 120$, $6! = 720$.

marginNotes.text:
He determines the number of the stars and 
calls them each by name.
